extra.message: User-controlled input `code` from `form.registration_code.data` is interpolated directly into a raw SQL string via an f-string passed to `session.execute(text(...))`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by providing a crafted registration code such as `' OR '1'='1`. This allows bypassing the registration code check, extracting database contents, or manipulating the query result.

User-controlled input `search_param` from `request.args.get('search', '')` is interpolated directly into a SQLAlchemy `text()` expression via an f-string: `text(f"text like '%{search_param}%'")`. This is passed as a filter predicate to `session.query(Note).filter(...)`. An attacker can inject SQL via the `search` query parameter, e.g., `%' UNION SELECT ... --`, allowing data exfiltration or manipulation.

The `preferences` cookie is base64-decoded and then passed directly to `pickle.loads()` in `before_request()` at line 118. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload, base64-encode it, and set it as their `preferences` cookie. When the server processes the next request from that cookie, `pickle.loads()` will execute arbitrary Python code, leading to remote code execution.

The `download()` function calls `urllib.request.urlopen(url)` with a URL provided directly by the user via `form.url.data` (ImageForm in routes/account.py). There is no validation of the URL scheme, host, or IP range. An attacker can supply internal URLs such as `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (cloud metadata) or `file:///etc/passwd` to make the server fetch internal resources, enabling SSRF.

In the 404 error handler, `render_template_string()` is called with an f-string that embeds `request.path` directly: `f"{error}. Requested URL was {request.path}"`. An attacker can craft a URL containing Jinja2 template syntax (e.g., `{{config}}` or `{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}`) which will be rendered by the template engine, potentially enabling full server-side template injection and remote code execution.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code (Code Injection)

extra.message: The `/accounts/<int:user_id>/notes` endpoint retrieves all notes for any `user_id` without verifying that `current_user.id == user_id`. Any authenticated user can enumerate notes belonging to other users by supplying a different `user_id` in the URL path. Private notes of other users are also returned, as there is no `private` field filter applied here.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The `/notes/<int:note_id>/delete` endpoint deletes a note by `note_id` without verifying that the note belongs to `current_user`. Any authenticated user can delete any other user's note by POST-ing to `/notes/<victim_note_id>/delete`. There is no ownership check (`note.user_id == current_user.id`) before calling `session.delete(note)`.

The `/account` POST endpoint (update_account) is not decorated with `@login_required`. More critically, `AccountForm` includes an `is_admin` BooleanField, and the handler calls `current_user.__dict__.update(filtered_values)` with all non-None form fields (excluding only `password`). Any user (or unauthenticated request targeting a logged-in session) can POST `is_admin=true` to promote themselves to admin. The `is_admin` field should not be user-modifiable.

The `update_account()` route handler for POST `/account` is missing the `@login_required` decorator, unlike the GET `/account` handler at line 18-21 which has it. An unauthenticated request that manages to POST to `/account` will run with `current_user` being an anonymous user object, leading to potential exceptions or state corruption.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Note content (`note.text`) is rendered with the Jinja2 `| safe` filter on line 31: `{{ note.text | safe }}`. This disables HTML auto-escaping for note text, which is populated from CKEditor (a rich text editor). An attacker who creates a note with a malicious HTML payload (e.g., `<script>document.cookie</script>`) will have that script executed in the browser of any user who views the home page — a stored XSS vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Stored XSS)

extra.message: The `render_table` macro is called with `safe_columns=['text']` on the `personal_notes` result set. This instructs flask-bootstrap to render the `text` column without HTML escaping. Note text comes from CKEditor (user-supplied HTML), so any stored XSS payload in a note's `text` field will be rendered as raw HTML in the search results table.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Stored XSS)

extra.message: The `render_table` macro is called with `safe_columns=['text']` on the `personal_notes` result set in the personal notes view. This disables HTML escaping for the `text` column. User-supplied note content containing JavaScript payloads will be executed as HTML in the viewer's browser.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Stored XSS)

extra.message: The Flask `secret_key` is hardcoded as the string `"super secret key"`. This key is used for signing session cookies and CSRF tokens. Any attacker who reads the source code (e.g., through a repository leak or the SSRF vulnerability present in this app) can forge session cookies, impersonate any user including admins, and bypass CSRF protections.

A static, hardcoded registration code `a36e990b-0024-4d55-b74a-f8d7528e1764` is always seeded into the database. Anyone with access to the source code can use this known code to register an account without a legitimate invitation, bypassing the intended access control on registration.

Seed users are created with trivially weak, hardcoded passwords: `user` for `user@evfa.com` and `admin` for `admin@evfa.com`. The admin account password is especially dangerous as it grants elevated privileges. These credentials are committed in source and would be trivially guessable in any deployed environment.

The SQLAlchemy engine is created with `echo=True`, which logs all SQL statements to stdout/stderr. In a production environment this exposes full SQL queries (including user data, query parameters, and potentially sensitive values) in application logs, leading to sensitive data exposure.

cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

extra.message: None of the state-changing POST endpoints in this application use CSRF tokens. The forms are plain WTForms (not Flask-WTF `FlaskForm`), so CSRF protection is not automatically applied. An attacker can host a page with a hidden form that POSTs to `/darkmode`, `/account`, `/notes`, `/account/image`, etc., causing authenticated users to unknowingly trigger state changes (e.g., self-promotion to admin via the `is_admin` field) when they visit the attacker's page.
